title: How to make tiffs where ITK from MONAI knows the pixel size?
decision: Save TIFF files with correct metadata (spacing, spacing_units, physical_size_x/y/z) for ITK/MONAI to read pixel size
rationale: Using OME-TIFF format with proper metadata extraction via MapTransform (OMEZSpacingFromFileD) to populate MONAI meta['spacing'] from file metadata. This avoids data duplication and ensures compatibility with ITK/MONAI transforms.
description: TIFF file metadata handling for pixel size (X,Y,Z) in MONAI/ITK pipeline, specifically for fluorescence microscopy data processing
cost: Implementation effort to modify TIFF saving functions and create MapTransform class for metadata extraction
risk: Potential compatibility issues between TIFF metadata formats and ITK/MONAI readers, requiring OME-TIFF standardization
author: joaomamede

arguments.content: Using OME-TIFF format with proper metadata extraction via MapTransform (OMEZSpacingFromFileD) is better than raw TIFF because it ensures pixel size metadata is properly read by ITK/MONAI without data duplication.
arguments.author: joaomamede

title: Default behaviour of `RandAffine` and `RandAffined`'s `scale_range` parameter adds a value of 1.0 to all elements, causing unintuitive scaling behavior.
decision: Change the `scale_range` parameter to use a scale where a value of 1.0 corresponds to no change.
rationale: The current behavior is unintuitive and has caused issues for users, as passing `[0.9, 1.1]` results in unexpected scaling (0.9+1.0=1.9 to 1.1+1.0=2.1) and downstream warnings in random cropping steps. The proposed change would align the parameter with user expectations.
description: The `scale_range` parameter in `RandAffine` and `RandAffined` classes in the MONAI library.
risk: The current behavior introduces unintuitive scaling (adding 1.0 to input values) which can lead to unexpected results and downstream issues (e.g., warnings in random cropping steps). The proposed change aims to eliminate this unintuitive behavior.
author: MathijsdeBoer

arguments.content: The current behavior of adding 1.0 to `scale_range` values is unintuitive and has caused issues for users, as passing `[0.9, 1.1]` results in unexpected scaling (0.9+1.0=1.9 to 1.1+1.0=2.1) and downstream warnings in random cropping steps. A value of 1.0 should correspond to no change.

A value of 1.0 should correspond to no change.

title: Detection model training with negative samples and anchor shape calculation for nodules < 3mm and >=3mm
decision: Including negative samples in training data using StandardizedEmptyBoxd transform
rationale: Negative samples inclusion improved training but validation accuracy remained zero for nodules >=3mm. Positive samples alone showed better validation accuracy after 80+ epochs. Suggests potential need to adjust base anchor shapes in config/train.json for better detection of smaller nodules.

title: Confusing behavior with the AsDiscrete transform where thresholding is applied after one-hot encoding, leading to unexpected results with probabilistic inputs.
decision: Apply thresholding before one-hot encoding in AsDiscrete transform to correctly map probabilistic outputs to discrete class indices.
rationale: The current implementation applies one-hot encoding first, which assumes discrete class indices as input, making it counterintuitive for probabilistic outputs. Applying thresholding before one-hot encoding aligns with expected behavior for post-processing model outputs.
description: AsDiscrete transform in MONAI library, specifically its behavior with probabilistic inputs and thresholding.
risk: Potential confusion and incorrect behavior in AsDiscrete transform when handling probabilistic outputs, leading to misinterpretation of model predictions.
author: thibaultdvx (issue reporter)

arguments.content: The current implementation applies one-hot encoding before thresholding, which is counterintuitive for probabilistic inputs. Applying thresholding first (e.g., using Compose([AsDiscrete(threshold=0.5), AsDiscrete(to_onehot=2, dim=1)])) produces the expected results.
arguments.author: thibaultdvx
arguments.argument: The current implementation applies one-hot encoding before thresholding, which is counterintuitive for probabilistic inputs. Applying thresholding first (e.g., using Compose([AsDiscrete(threshold=0.5), AsDiscrete(to_onehot=2, dim=1)])) produces the expected results.

arguments.content: The current implementation of AsDiscrete first applies one-hot encoding and then performs thresholding, which assumes discrete class indices as input. This is counterintuitive for probabilistic outputs like softmax or sigmoid values, where thresholding should be applied before one-hot encoding.

title: Difference between self-attention and cross-attention in diffusion model U-Net
rationale: Cross-attention is better when conditioning input has more structure (clinical features, text, etc.), as it allows the network to focus dynamically instead of treating the label as a simple embedding. Self-attention is sufficient for a few discrete labels.
description: Training a 2D conditional diffusion model on different labels with U-Net architecture

arguments.author: NabJa
arguments.argument: Cross-attention is really good when your conditioning input has more structure (clinical features, text, etc.), since it lets the network focus dynamically instead of treating the label as a simple embedding.

title: Feature request to add SparseDice, SparseClDice, CenterlineDice Losses and 3D TransUNet for 3D medical image segmentation tasks
decision: Addition of SparseDiceLoss, SparseClDiceLoss, CenterlineDiceLoss, and 3D TransUNet to MONAI
rationale: These additions would greatly help users working on complex 3D segmentation tasks, especially with sparse annotations and tubular structures. SparseDiceLoss supports sparse labels for multi-class segmentation with incomplete annotations. SparseClDiceLoss combines clDice and sparse label support for tubular structures. CenterlineDiceLoss measures topological agreement between predicted and ground-truth structures by comparing their soft skeletons, useful for thin elongated objects in 2D/3D. 3D TransUNet extends the current 2D TransUNet to full 3D to handle volumetric medical images directly.
description: MONAI library for 3D medical image segmentation tasks
author: jermmy19998

title: Current MONAI segmentation metrics aggregate scores globally, masking clinically relevant failure modes like missing small lesions while achieving high Dice scores due to dominant large structures. There is no native support for connected component aware evaluation except Panoptic Quality.
decision: Add connected component aware variants of existing MONAI semantic segmentation metrics (CC Metrics).
rationale: The proposed CC Metrics operate as thin wrappers around existing MONAI metrics to decompose ground truth foreground into connected components, assign voxels via Voronoi partition, evaluate standard MONAI metrics per component, and support aggregation modes. This addresses the issue of masking clinically relevant failure modes in multi-instance medical segmentation tasks.

title: AutoencoderKlMaisi transfers to cuda even if input and model are on CPU
decision: Fix the transfer to CUDA when input and autoencoder are on CPU
rationale: The issue occurs because the code unconditionally transfers tensors to CUDA in the _concatenate_tensors method, even when the input and model are on CPU. The fix should ensure tensors remain on CPU when the input and model are CPU-based.
description: The _concatenate_tensors method in monai/apps/generation/maisi/networks/autoencoderkl_maisi.py, specifically around line 228 where the tensor transfer to CUDA occurs.
cost: High (requires fixing the unconditional CUDA transfer which causes OOM errors on systems with limited GPU memory)
risk: Technical debt: Unconditional CUDA transfer causes out-of-memory errors when GPU memory is insufficient. Potential side effects include unexpected GPU memory usage and crashes in CPU-only environments.
author: coolteemf

arguments.content: The current implementation unconditionally transfers tensors to CUDA (line 228: `x = x.to("cuda", non_blocking=True)`), which causes out-of-memory errors when the input and model are on CPU but the system has limited GPU memory. The fix should check the device of the input and model before transferring to CUDA.

title: apex.contrib.clip_grad.clip_grad_norm_ crashes with PyTorch 2.10 due to multi_tensor_applier attempting to access raw data pointer of gradient tensors, which no longer expose traditional storage in PyTorch 2.10
decision: Upgrade apex.contrib.clip_grad.clip_grad_norm_ to be compatible with PyTorch 2.10's tensor storage changes
rationale: PyTorch 2.10 introduced changes where some tensors (e.g., those with lazy/functional storage) no longer expose traditional storage, causing apex's multi_tensor_applier to fail when accessing raw data pointers of gradient tensors. This necessitates an update to apex.contrib.clip_grad.clip_grad_norm_ to handle these new tensor storage behaviors.

title: Bug in `make_gaussian_kernel` causes incorrect `LocalNormalizedCrossCorrelationLoss` when `kernel_size > 3`
decision: Fix the `truncated` parameter in `make_gaussian_kernel` to be passed as a multiple of sigma (e.g., 1.0) instead of absolute pixel radius
rationale: The bug occurs because `make_gaussian_kernel` incorrectly passes the absolute pixel radius (`kernel_size // 2`) to `gaussian_1d`'s `truncated` parameter, which expects truncation in units of standard deviations. This causes the kernel values to become negligibly small, corrupting the LNCC calculation.
description: The `make_gaussian_kernel` function in `monai/losses/image_dissimilarity.py` and its impact on `LocalNormalizedCrossCorrelationLoss` with `kernel_type="gaussian"` and `kernel_size > 3`
cost: Low (requires correction of a parameter misconfiguration in the kernel generation function)
risk: High (numerical instability in LNCC calculation due to extremely small kernel values, leading to incorrect loss values)
author: xiyizhou16

arguments.content: The `truncated` parameter in `make_gaussian_kernel` is incorrectly set to `kernel_size // 2` (absolute pixel radius) instead of a multiple of sigma (standard deviations), causing the Gaussian kernel to be truncated at an excessively large radius (e.g., 7 sigma for kernel_size=15). This results in negligibly small kernel values, corrupting the LNCC calculation.

arguments.argument: The `truncated` parameter in `make_gaussian_kernel` is incorrectly set to `kernel_size // 2` (absolute pixel radius) instead of a multiple of sigma (standard deviations), causing the Gaussian kernel to be truncated at an excessively large radius (e.g., 7 sigma for kernel_size=15). This results in negligibly small kernel values, corrupting the LNCC calculation.

title: Medical image segmentation models trained with overlap-based loss functions like Dice and Tversky are susceptible to class imbalance, particularly when the foreground region is small relative to the background. These losses only consider TP, FP, and FN from the confusion matrix, ignoring true negatives (TN), which means they do not penalize misclassifications of background pixels.
decision: Add a Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) loss function to monai.losses.
rationale: The MCC loss uses all four entries of the confusion matrix (TP, TN, FP, FN) and has been shown to be effective for class-imbalanced segmentation. The loss is computed as 1 - MCC where MCC = (TP * TN - FP * FN) / sqrt((TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)), using soft confusion matrix entries to make it differentiable. This was proposed in the paper 'Matthews Correlation Coefficient Loss for Deep Convolutional Networks: Application to Skin Lesion Segmentation' by K. Abhishek and G. Hamarneh.
description: monai.losses (specifically for the MCC loss function)
risk: Class imbalance issues in medical image segmentation models trained with overlap-based loss functions like Dice and Tversky, particularly when the foreground region is small relative to the background.

title: Silent loss of discriminative power in model embeddings (representational collapse) when fine-tuning or deploying foundation models on new domains, leading to misleading downstream task performance.
decision: Implementation of `EmbeddingCollapseMetric`, a new metric class to detect representational collapse in medical imaging embeddings.
rationale: The metric computes a suite of collapse indicators (centroid cosine similarity, SVD effective rank score, per-class SVD, Linear CKA, Silhouette score, leave-one-out centroid stability) to quantitatively detect representational collapse and validate point estimates on small sample sizes. It addresses the gap where users currently have no way to determine if embeddings are separating classes meaningfully or have degenerated into a narrow subspace.
description: Medical imaging embeddings in foundation models, specifically addressing representational collapse during fine-tuning or deployment on new domains.
cost: Low (core dependencies are torch and numpy; sklearn and matplotlib are optional and imported lazily)
risk: Potential silent degradation of model performance due to representational collapse, leading to misleading downstream task performance that only becomes apparent after costly training cycles. Risk of asymmetric collapse where one class collapses while others remain rich.
author: ekansh-arora0

arguments.content: FIDMetric measures distributional shift but requires a large reference set and doesn't decompose by class or detect dimensional collapse. MMDMetric is a two-sample test, not a collapse detector. `EmbeddingCollapseMetric` is complementary to both, answering a different question: not 'are these distributions different?' but 'has the model lost the ability to distinguish classes?'

title: The docstring examples in `SSIMLoss.forward()` incorrectly show `1-SSIMLoss()(x,y)`, which negates the loss because `SSIMLoss.forward()` already computes `1 - ssim_value` internally. This leads users to compute a 'loss' that increases when images become less similar, training in the wrong direction.
decision: Remove the `1-` prefix from each of the three docstring example `print()` statements in `SSIMLoss.forward()` to correctly compute the loss.
rationale: The current docstring examples incorrectly suggest subtracting 1 from the result of `SSIMLoss()(x,y)`, but the loss is already computed as `1 - ssim_value` internally. Removing the `1-` prefix aligns the examples with the actual implementation, ensuring users compute the correct loss value for training.
description: The docstring examples in `SSIMLoss.forward()` (file: `monai/losses/ssim_loss.py`, ~line 100) and the `SSIMLoss` class implementation.
risk: Users following the incorrect docstring examples would compute a 'loss' that increases when images become less similar, leading to incorrect training direction. This introduces a risk of training failure or degraded model performance due to the opposite optimization direction.
author: Zeesejo

arguments.content: The current docstring examples incorrectly show `1-SSIMLoss()(x,y)`, but `SSIMLoss.forward()` already computes `1 - ssim_value` internally, so calling `1-SSIMLoss()(x,y)` produces `1 - (1 - ssim) = ssim`, which is the structural similarity value — not the loss.

title: Fix using a non-tuple sequence for multidimensional indexing that will result in error in PyTorch 2.9
decision: Replace list- or slice-based indices with tuples using ensure_tuple in monai/inferers/utils.py
rationale: Using ensure_tuple ensures compatibility with PyTorch 2.9+ by converting sequences to tuples for safe multidimensional indexing. This approach standardizes internal indexing in sliding-window inference, covering buffered and non-buffered write paths, per-slice handling for sw_batch_size variants, and _compute_coords. Tests verified parity between buffered and non-buffered flows and confirmed _compute_coords accepts list-based indices.

risk: Low (no new risks introduced; addresses PyTorch 2.9+ compatibility issue)

title: Division by zero in clDice loss harmonic mean when computing topology precision and sensitivity
decision: Add epsilon (1e-7) to the denominator (tprec + tsens) in clDice loss computation
rationale: Prevents NaN when both tprec and tsens are zero (e.g., empty inputs, non-overlapping predictions/targets, or smooth=0). Returns cl_dice = 1.0 (maximum loss) when both precision and sensitivity are zero, which is the correct semantic result. Has negligible impact on normal computation (tprec, tsens are bounded in [0, 1]). Consistent with epsilon-based denominator guards used elsewhere in MONAI (e.g., smooth_dr in DiceLoss)
description: clDice loss computation in SoftclDiceLoss and SoftDiceclDiceLoss
cost: Negligible (epsilon value of 1e-7 added to denominator)
risk: Potential NaN propagation through loss and training crashes when both tprec and tsens are zero without the fix
author: Mr-Neutr0n

arguments.content: Suggests following the pattern found with other Dice losses by adding a smooth_dr constructor argument with a value of 1e-7 instead of hardcoding the epsilon value in the forward methods

title: Support for additional dtypes (bool and integer) in the pad_nd function due to removal of hardcoded dtype exclusion list
decision: Remove hardcoded dtype exclusion list in pad_nd function to support more dtypes
rationale: Previously, _pt_pad was restricted to specific dtypes, now it is used for all dtypes when the padding mode is in the allowed set, improving flexibility and compatibility with PyTorch's optimized padding
description: pad_nd function in the MONAI library
author: skdas20

arguments.content: Previously, _pt_pad was used only if the mode was in {"constant", "reflect", "edge", "replicate", "wrap", "circular"} and the tensor dtype was not in {torch.int16, torch.int64, torch.bool, torch.uint8}. Now _pt_pad is chosen whenever the mode is in that set, regardless of dtype, which removes unnecessary restrictions and allows for more flexible dtype support.

title: Add GradientAccumulation utility for SupervisedTrainer to enable gradient accumulation over multiple mini-batches for memory-constrained hardware
decision: Add GradientAccumulation callable class in monai.engines.utils for use as iteration_update in SupervisedTrainer
rationale: Gradient accumulation enables simulating larger effective batch sizes on memory-constrained hardware by accumulating gradients over multiple mini-batches. The implementation follows the callable-class iteration_update pattern established by Interaction in monai.apps.deepedit. Mixed-precision (GradScaler) support is included to maintain compatibility with existing training workflows.

title: Silently incorrect spatial transform results on NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs (compute capability 12.x, sm_120) when MONAI is run with USE_COMPILED=True due to mismatched CUDA architectures in the compiled C extension.
decision: Add a private helper `_compiled_unsupported(device: torch.device) -> bool` to detect unsupported CUDA devices (compute capability major ≥ 12) and gate the compiled spatial resampling path, falling back to PyTorch-native `affine_grid + grid_sample` path on unsupported devices.
rationale: The compiled path for `spatial_resample` and `Warp` is now gated on `USE_COMPILED and not _compiled_unsupported(img.device)` to avoid incorrect results on Blackwell GPUs. Additionally, `Warp.__init__` now stores both compiled integer modes and native string modes to ensure correct fallback path execution.
description: Spatial transforms (`spatial_resample`, `Resample`, `Warp`) and CUDA device detection in MONAI's compiled C extension (`monai._C`).
cost: Low to moderate (implementation of device detection and fallback logic with minimal changes to existing code paths)
risk: Technical debt from maintaining separate code paths for compiled and native spatial transforms. Potential side effects from fallback behavior changes in `spatial_resample` and `Warp`.
author: garciadias

arguments.content: Blackwell GPUs (sm_120) are not included in the default CUDA architectures build list (sm_75 through sm_90), causing `grid_pull` to execute against a mismatched PTX or JIT path and silently return wrong values.
arguments.author: garciadias
arguments.argument: Blackwell GPUs (sm_120) are not included in the default CUDA architectures build list (sm_75 through sm_90), causing `grid_pull` to execute against a mismatched PTX or JIT path and silently return wrong values.

arguments.content: Tolerance tests are passing, confirming the fix resolves the issue without breaking existing functionality.

title: Add support for connected component-based Dice metric calculation to the existing DiceMetric and DiceHelper classes
decision: Add per_component parameter to DiceMetric and DiceHelper classes
rationale: To compute Dice scores for each connected component individually, enabling more granular evaluation of segmentation quality. title: Fix multi-axis shear transform to compose individual shear matrices
decision: Refactor _create_shear function to compose individual single-axis shear matrices rather than directly assigning off-diagonal elements

title: Add Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) Loss
decision: Add Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) loss function to monai.losses
rationale: MCC loss considers all four entries of the confusion matrix (TP, TN, FP, FN), making it effective for class-imbalanced segmentation tasks. The loss was proposed in Abhishek & Hamarneh (IEEE ISBI 2021), has been cited 75 times, and has been adopted by Segmentation Models PyTorch (smp). The implementation follows MONAI conventions, supporting sigmoid, softmax, to_onehot_y, include_background, batch, and reduction parameters.

title: Fix handling of `proj_attn`→`out_proj` key mapping in `load_old_state_dict` for backward compatibility in AutoEncoderKL
decision: Map `proj_attn` → `out_proj` when both exist, initialize `out_proj` to identity/zero when only the new model has it, and silently discard `proj_attn` when only the old checkpoint has it
rationale: Ensure backward compatibility by handling legacy attention projection keys (`proj_attn.weight`/`proj_attn.bias`) per-block in AutoEncoderKL.load_old_state_dict. If the new model has `attn.out_proj.*` and legacy `proj_attn.*` exist, copy them (initialize missing legacy bias to zeros with matching dtype/device). If the new model lacks `attn.out_proj.*` but legacy `proj_attn.*` exist, remove those legacy keys from the old state dict to avoid unexpected-key errors. The method no longer unconditionally sets `out_proj.weight` to identity/ bias to zero.
description: AutoEncoderKL.load_old_state_dict method in the AutoEncoderKL class
cost: Moderate (estimated code review effort: ~20 minutes)
risk: Potential risks include breaking backward compatibility if the key mapping logic is incorrect, or introducing unexpected errors if legacy keys are not properly handled (e.g., unexpected-key errors or incorrect weight initialization).

title: Need to enable global coordinates in spatial crop transforms to avoid manual conversion of annotations from physical to voxel space when preprocessing spacing changes
decision: Add `TransformPointsWorldToImaged` and `TransformPointsImageToWorldd` transforms and enable string dictionary keys for `roi_center`, `roi_size`, `roi_start`, and `roi_end` in `SpatialCropd`
rationale: Eliminates manual recalculation of voxel coordinates by allowing direct use of world-space annotations in cropping pipelines. Zero overhead for existing usage as the original code path is preserved when no string keys are passed. String-key path defers slice computation until data dict is available, ensuring dynamic resolution of coordinates.
description: Spatial crop transforms in MONAI's preprocessing pipeline, specifically affecting `SpatialCropd`, `TransformPointsWorldToImaged`, and `TransformPointsImageToWorldd`
cost: Minimal overhead for existing usage; string-key path recreates `SpatialCrop` on each `__call__` (stored on `self.cropper`), which may introduce computational complexity but ensures dynamic resolution of coordinates.
risk: Potential technical debt from deferred slice computation in string-key path of `SpatialCropd`; requires careful handling of `inverse()` to avoid inconsistencies in transform stack. Risk of systematic bias in coordinate rounding via `torch.round` (banker's rounding).
author: emanuilo

arguments.content: Using world-space annotations directly in cropping pipelines (via `TransformPointsWorldToImaged` and `SpatialCropd` string keys) is better than manual conversion to voxel space because it eliminates error-prone recalculation steps and ensures consistency across preprocessing changes.
arguments.author: emanuilo

title: Handling precomputed crops and safe no-grad cleanup in auto3dseg analyzer
decision: Added validation for precomputed nda_croppeds and used @torch.no_grad() for exception-safe gradient state handling in ImageStats, FgImageStats, and LabelStats
rationale: Added explicit type and length validation for precomputed nda_croppeds to raise clear ValueError for invalid/mismatched values. Used @torch.no_grad() to ensure gradient state is preserved after exceptions, addressing potential side effects and uncertainties in the architecture.
description: monai.auto3dseg.analyzer (ImageStats, FgImageStats, LabelStats)
cost: Minor (added tests and validation logic)
risk: Potential side effects from gradient state leakage if no-grad context was not properly handled. Technical debt from unclear error messages for malformed precomputed crops.
author: bluehyena

arguments.content: The ternary in nda_croppeds handling lacks validation; errors surface downstream as confusing shape mismatches rather than clear messages. #8809 adds explicit type and length validation with descriptive ValueError.
arguments.author: williams145
arguments.argument: The ternary in nda_croppeds handling lacks validation; errors surface downstream as confusing shape mismatches rather than clear messages. #8809 adds explicit type and length validation with descriptive ValueError.

arguments.content: torch.no_grad() always restores grad to enabled after the block, regardless of caller state. If caller had grad disabled, this silently re-enables it, a subtle semantic change. #8810 uses try/finally with torch.is_grad_enabled() to restore exact pre-call state.
arguments.author: williams145
arguments.argument: torch.no_grad() always restores grad to enabled after the block, regardless of caller state. If caller had grad disabled, this silently re-enables it, a subtle semantic change. #8810 uses try/finally with torch.is_grad_enabled() to restore exact pre-call state.

arguments.content: Moved ImageStats.__call__ docstring to method top and switched analyzer __call__ methods to @torch.no_grad() for clarity. Verified no_grad restores caller's previous grad state on exit, including exception paths.
arguments.author: bluehyena
arguments.argument: Moved ImageStats.__call__ docstring to method top and switched analyzer __call__ methods to @torch.no_grad() for clarity. Verified no_grad restores caller's previous grad state on exit, including exception paths.

arguments.content: Correction: torch.no_grad() does not unconditionally re-enable gradients on exit; __exit__ calls torch.set_grad_enabled(self.prev), preserving caller's gradient state.
arguments.author: williams145
arguments.argument: Correction: torch.no_grad() does not unconditionally re-enable gradients on exit; __exit__ calls torch.set_grad_enabled(self.prev), preserving caller's gradient state.

arguments.content: nda_croppeds validation addresses earlier concerns about unclear error messages for malformed precomputed values.
arguments.author: williams145
arguments.argument: nda_croppeds validation addresses earlier concerns about unclear error messages for malformed precomputed values.

title: Fix make_gaussian_kernel truncated parameter for kernel_size > 3
decision: Set truncated = (kernel_size // 2) / sigma in make_gaussian_kernel
rationale: The original implementation passed kernel_size // 2 directly to gaussian_1d, which interprets truncated as standard deviations, not pixel radius. This caused incorrect kernel sizing (e.g., kernel_size=15, sigma=5.0, truncated=7 resulted in 71-element kernel instead of 15). The fix normalizes truncated by dividing by sigma, converting pixel radius to standard deviation units before passing to gaussian_1d.

title: PyTorch 2.9 compatibility issue with torch.Tensor input in compute_shape_offset function
decision: Wrap spatial_shape in tuple() before passing to np.array() to ensure compatibility with PyTorch tensors
rationale: The direct caller in monai/transforms/spatial/functional.py constructs an in_spatial_size as a torch.Tensor and passes it to compute_shape_offset, which relies on non-tuple sequence indexing protocol removed in PyTorch 2.9. Wrapping spatial_shape in tuple() routes through __iter__, which is stable across PyTorch versions, and produces 0-d scalar tensors that NumPy consumes correctly.

title: Forced eager C-order copy in NibabelReader._get_array_data causes unnecessary memory reordering during NIfTI file loading, leading to performance issues reported in issue #8107.
decision: Remove forced C-order conversion in NibabelReader._get_array_data to allow nibabel's native (F-order) memory layout.
rationale: The forced C-order conversion was causing a full dense memory reorder on every load, adding unnecessary overhead to the hot path. Dropping it aligns the CPU path with the existing GPU/cupy branch and avoids materializing nibabel's memmap view unnecessarily. Downstream MONAI conversion paths already handle C-contiguous memory where needed.
description: NibabelReader._get_array_data method in the NibabelReader class, specifically in the file image_reader.py.

cost: Performance improvement: Avoids full dense memory reordering on every load, reducing overhead in the hot path. Biggest wins on uncompressed .nii files where nibabel's memmap view is returned lazily rather than materialized.
risk: Potential compatibility issues for external callers consuming reader.get_data(...)[0] directly via .tobytes() or raw C-extension buffer without first calling ascontiguousarray, as the returned array's memory layout changes from C-contiguous to F-contiguous.

versions.decision: Remove forced C-order conversion in NibabelReader._get_array_data to allow nibabel's native (F-order) memory layout.
versions.rationale: The forced C-order conversion was causing a full dense memory reorder on every load, adding unnecessary overhead to the hot path. Dropping it aligns the CPU path with the existing GPU/cupy branch and avoids materializing nibabel's memmap view unnecessarily. Downstream MONAI conversion paths already handle C-contiguous memory where needed.
versions.context: NibabelReader._get_array_data method in the NibabelReader class, specifically in the file image_reader.py.
versions.cost: Performance improvement: Avoids full dense memory reordering on every load, reducing overhead in the hot path. Biggest wins on uncompressed .nii files where nibabel's memmap view is returned lazily rather than materialized.
versions.risk: Potential compatibility issues for external callers consuming reader.get_data(...)[0] directly via .tobytes() or raw C-extension buffer without first calling ascontiguousarray, as the returned array's memory layout changes from C-contiguous to F-contiguous.

title: Input spatial dimensions must be divisible by 2 ** (num_depths + int(use_downsample)) for DiNTS
decision: Added input-size validation to DiNTS: the constructor now checks that a provided `dints_space` object's `use_downsample` attribute matches the `use_downsample` argument and raises `ValueError` on mismatch. Introduced a private `@torch.jit.unused` helper `DiNTS._check_input_size(spatial_shape)` that computes the required divisibility factor `2 ** (num_depths + int(use_downsample))` and raises `ValueError` listing offending spatial dimensions. `DiNTS.forward(x)` calls this helper at runtime when not scripting/tracing.
rationale: Previously only a `print()` warning existed in `TopologyConstruction.__init__` — no error was raised. This PR adds `_check_input_size()` to `DiNTS`, calls it in `forward()`, updates the class docstring with a `Spatial Shape Constraints` section, and adds tests for both 3D and 2D invalid inputs to enforce spatial shape constraints and improve robustness.

title: Fix SSIMLoss docstring examples to remove redundant 1- prefix in print statements, ensuring examples print the actual loss value (1 minus SSIM index) rather than the similarity value.
decision: Remove the redundant 1- prefix in the three print(1-SSIMLoss(...)(x,y)) calls in the docstring examples to make them print the actual loss value.
rationale: The docstring examples were printing 1 - (1 - ssim) = ssim (similarity value) instead of the loss (1 - ssim) due to the redundant 1- prefix. Removing the 1- prefix aligns the examples with the Returns docstring, which states the loss is '1 minus the ssim index'. This ensures users copying the examples would not train in the wrong direction.
description: SSIMLoss.forward() docstring examples in monai/losses/ssim_loss.py
risk: Risk of users copying the docstring examples and building a 'loss' that increases as images grow more similar, leading to incorrect training direction.
author: haoyu-haoyu

title: PatchEmbeddingBlock with proj_type='perceptron' raises TypeError on einops >= 0.8.x due to Rearrange.__init__() no longer accepting arbitrary keyword arguments
decision: Embed patch size values directly as integer literals in the einops pattern string (e.g., 'b c (h 16) (w 16) (d 16)') instead of using kwargs
rationale: einops supports integer literals in patterns across all versions including 0.8.x, making the change semantically identical to the original implementation
description: PatchEmbeddingBlock in MONAI's codebase, specifically the proj_type='perceptron' implementation
cost: Low (non-breaking change fix with minimal code modification)
risk: Technical debt from maintaining compatibility with multiple einops versions, potential side effects from pattern string changes
author: williams145

arguments.content: Introduces a new PyTorch module _PatchRearrange that converts inputs shaped (B, C, ...spatial...) into a sequence (B, n_patches, patch_dim) using explicit view/permute/reshape. In PatchEmbeddingBlock.__init__ for proj_type == 'perceptron', construction of the Rearrage layer is wrapped in a try block; if einops.Rearrange raises a TypeError, code falls back to _PatchRearrange(spatial_dims, patch_size) before applying nn.Linear(self.patch_dim, hidden_size). The original Rearrage -> Linear path is preserved when no error occurs.

title: LoadImage with ensure_channel_first=True raises ValueError when set_track_meta(False) is active
decision: pass meta_data explicitly to EnsureChannelFirst when img is not a MetaTensor
rationale: EnsureChannelFirst.__call__ already accepts a meta_dict parameter; meta_data is always a validated dict at this point in LoadImage.__call__
description: LoadImage.__call__ method in LoadImage class
cost: Trivial (Estimated code review effort)
risk: Crash when set_track_meta(False) is used alongside ensure_channel_first=True
author: williams145

arguments.content: Modified LoadImage.__call__ to conditionally handle channel-first conversion based on whether the loaded image is a MetaTensor. When ensure_channel_first is enabled, the code now checks the image type: if not a MetaTensor, it passes the metadata dictionary to EnsureChannelFirst; if already a MetaTensor, it calls EnsureChannelFirst without the metadata argument. This addresses crashes that occur when set_track_meta(False) is used alongside ensure_channel_first=True.
